mappings.rationale: Directly covers the scientific study of human behaviour and experience, including cognitive, conative, and affective dimensions.
Directly addresses conscious experience, awareness, and the nature of human behavioural capacities held to be distinctive of humans.
Directly relevant to the evolutionary development of mankind and phylogenetic continuity between humans and other primates (section A.1.c).
Covers evolutionary theory underlying the explanation of distinctive human traits in accordance with phylogenetic continuity (section A.1.c).
Directly covers stages of human development including infancy, early childhood, and childhood (sections B.4, B.5).
Directly covers the adolescence stage of human development from puberty to adulthood (section B.6).
Directly covers the affective dimension of behaviour including emotions, the pleasant and unpleasant, and sentiments (section A.3.c).
Relevant to cognitive dimensions of behaviour, the nature-nurture controversy regarding genetic endowment vs. environmental factors (section A.2).
Relevant to emergent problems in human experience and altered states of consciousness.
Directly addresses the voluntary and involuntary aspects of the conative dimension (section A.3.b) and human agency.
Directly relevant to understanding behavioral capacities humans share with other primates and higher mammals (section A.1.a).
Directly addresses the comparison of human and artificial, machine-created intelligence (section A.5).
Covers cultural conditioning and personal socialization experiences as environmental conditions shaping behaviour (section A.2.b).
Relevant to the nature-nurture controversy and elements of genetic endowment including physiological and psychological characteristics (section A.2.a).
Covers the genetic basis of the nature-nurture question, including reflexes, instincts, and physiological characteristics (section A.2.a).
Covers the neural basis of cognitive, conative, and affective dimensions of behaviour and the biological substrate of human experience.
Covers the neural foundations of sensation, perception, memory, concept formation, and reasoning (section A.3.a).
Directly covers concept formation, ideation, and reasoning as cognitive dimensions of behaviour and experience (section A.3.a).
Directly covers memory and imagination as key aspects of the cognitive dimension of behaviour (section A.3.a).
Directly covers sensation and perception as cognitive dimensions of behaviour and experience (section A.3.a).
Covers prenatal growth and development (section B.2) and the biological basis of human developmental stages.
Propositional language is identified as a distinctively human behavioral capacity (section A.1.b), making linguistics directly relevant.
Relevant to propositional language as a distinctive human trait and the role of language in cumulative cultural transmission (section A.1.b).
Covers environmental conditions including cultural conditioning and personal socialization experiences that shape behaviour (section A.2.b).
Addresses fundamental questions about the relationship between mind and body, consciousness, and the nature of human experience.
Addresses whether mankind is radically distinct from or continuous with nature (section A.1) and the philosophical implications of evolutionary continuity.
Relevant to the affective dimension of behaviour and experience, including emotions and the pleasant and unpleasant (section A.3.c).
Relevant to behavioral capacities held to be distinctive of humans and the cognitive dimensions of ideation and imagination.
Directly addresses questions of human nature, personal identity, and the subjective dimensions of experience and behaviour.
Relevant to the comparison of human and machine intelligence (section A.5) and the question of human distinctiveness.
Relevant to the biological elements of genetic endowment that shape physiological characteristics and behavioral patterns (section A.2.a).
